title: Unstable Angina
chief_complaint: Chest pressure started at rest this morning. It's different from my usual. It lasted 20 minutes and went away on its own. Now it's back.

physical_exam.Cardiac: Regular tachycardia, no murmurs. S4 present.

hidden_data.ecg: Sinus tachycardia 94 bpm. ST depression 1mm in leads V4-V6. T wave inversion in leads I, aVL. No ST elevation.
hidden_data.cxr: Normal cardiac size. Mild pulmonary vascular congestion.
hidden_data.echo: Mild hypokinesis of anterior wall. EF 50%.

correct_diagnosis: Unstable Angina / Non-ST Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome (NSTE-ACS)
key_learning_points: Unstable angina is defined as new-onset angina, angina at rest, or crescendo angina (increasing frequency/duration)
It is an ACS equivalent requiring urgent hospitalization, antiplatelet therapy, and cardiology consultation
ECG may show ST depression, T wave inversion, or be normal in UA. It does NOT show ST elevation (that would be STEMI)
Negative troponin differentiates UA from NSTEMI. UA has lower mortality but still requires aggressive management
GRACE score stratifies risk and guides management in NSTE-ACS

patient_responses.pain details: It woke me up this morning. Pressure in the center of my chest. It went away for 2 hours and now it's back but milder.
patient_responses.compared to usual: I've had chest discomfort before when I exercise, but never at rest. This feels more intense and scary.
patient_responses.risk factors: I have diabetes type 2, high blood pressure. Smoked for 20 years, quit 5 years ago. My brother had a heart attack at 55.

hints.history: Distinguish between stable vs unstable angina. Key: rest pain, new onset, or crescendo pattern = unstable.
hints.examination: Look for signs of heart failure (S3, crackles, JVD). Assess hemodynamic stability.
hints.labs: Serial troponin q3-6h. If negative ×2, UA confirmed. If elevated, NSTEMI. GRACE score for risk stratification.
hints.imaging: Echo to assess LV function and wall motion. Cath within 24h for high-risk features.

feedback.correct_message: Correct diagnosis! This is classic unstable angina - rest pain with dynamic ST-T changes but negative troponins. You recognized this as an ACS equivalent requiring urgent management and cardiology consultation.
feedback.partially_correct_message: You're close! This is unstable angina, not stable angina. The key differentiating factors are: pain at rest, longer duration (20 min), and ST-T changes on ECG. This requires hospitalization.
feedback.incorrect_message: This presentation is unstable angina/NSTE-ACS: rest pain >20 minutes with dynamic ECG changes. Even with negative troponins, this is an emergency requiring antiplatelet therapy, anticoagulation, and urgent cardiology consultation.
clinical_pearls: Unstable angina and NSTEMI are on the same spectrum - differentiated only by troponin elevation
GRACE score >140 indicates high risk requiring early invasive strategy (cath within 24h)
DAPT (Aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor) is essential in ACS management

ecg.comment: ST depression in V4-V6 with T inversion indicates anterior ischemia - high-risk finding

troponin_serial.comment: Negative troponins differentiate UA from NSTEMI. Must check 3h delta

echo.comment: Wall motion abnormality confirms ischemia. EF guides management

cxr.comment: Shows pulmonary congestion suggesting LV dysfunction

missing_tests: GRACE score calculation - Stratifies risk and guides invasive vs conservative strategy
feedback.attending_note: Doctor, this is unstable angina - an ACS equivalent. Start Aspirin 324mg chewed, Ticagrelor 180mg loading dose, Enoxaparin 1mg/kg SC, and high-intensity statin. Cardiology consult for urgent catheterization within 24 hours given high-risk features (ST depression, elevated GRACE score, diabetes).
